hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Italia' — the -am ending marks the accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Italia' — 'Italiam' with -am is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Italiae' ends in -ae (1st declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Italiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'Italiae' ends in -ae

steps.reveal: primus = first (nom. masc. sg., modifying Achates)

hints.accusative: Accusative masc. sg. would be 'primum' — 'primus' with -us is nominative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'primi' — 'primus' is nominative, agreeing with 'Achates'
hints.vocative: Vocative of 2nd declension -us words is -e ('prime') — 'primus' is nominative

hints.to close: That would be 'claudere' — 'conclamat' comes from 'conclamare' (to shout out)
hints.to call together: That would be 'convocare' — 'conclamare' means to shout out/cry aloud
hints.to conceal: That would be 'celare' — 'conclamat' comes from 'conclamare' (to shout out)

steps.reveal: conclamat = shouts out (historic present for vividness)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'conclamavit' — 'conclamat' is present indicative
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'conclamabat' — 'conclamat' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'conclamet' — 'conclamat' with -a- is present indicative (1st conj.)

hints.claudo, claudere, clausi, clausum: These are the parts of 'claudere' (to close/shut)
hints.clamo, clamare, clamavi, clamatum: These are the parts of 'clamare' (to shout) — close, but 'conclamare' is the compound
hints.convoco, convocare, convocavi, convocatu: These are the parts of 'convocare' (to call together)

hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Achate' or 'Achata' — 'Achates' is nominative, the subject
hints.accusative: Accusative of Greek names in -es is often -en — 'Achates' is nominative here
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Achatae' — 'Achates' is nominative singular

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Achatae' ends in -ae (1st decl. Greek masculine)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Achatae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Achatae' ends in -ae

translation: Italy! Italy! Achates first shouts out
